item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this SCP-J concept presented as a mainlist article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a sucker for any SCP where we normal people in the real world are the confused ones. +1

item.title: DO IT!
item.content:encoded: Take my upvote and make them

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'm with the others with problems here. Of note is the way that you handle some story threads. For example, the idea that some people are psychically resistant, that the Foundation discovered them through a wresting anomaly, and that they're using it to forcibly recruit people, is a pretty cool narrative with a lot of twists that could be doled out across many logs/sections. Here, it's dumped out in the containment and makes the description harder to read.

Then there's the question of the wrestlers themselves. In the current version, they know what's going on and are clearly highly violent individuals. I got a very strong and cool mental image of that from the picture of the wrestler with the knives in his back, but the slight personality change in the interview didn't really match up with the level of changes in the matches. It's not a particularly strange difference either, but still too evident to be subtly creepy.

Then you address the consequences by showing one wrestler's death, but that brings up an important question: how is everyone else surviving this and still doing regular shows? I admit I've only seen a bit of wrestling, but are there really enough changes and events to cover up for healing times and fatalities on this level.

(Also, minor point, when you're having two people "independently" transcribe events, they probably wouldn't share entire paragraphs word for word.)

(Kayfabe also has a long history and the nature of it in pop culture has significantly changed even within my lifetime (the Montreal Screwjob comes to mind). I am absolutely not asking you to include an essay on the history of WWF meta-narative: this anomaly almost exists independent from actual kayfabe, but a complicated reality plus sparse/sketchy conception of the anomaly make this SCP… messy.)

—
Overall, I agree with the others that the central concept of Kayfabe as SCP wasn't pushed hard enough into its logical or bizarre conclusions. You've got several anomalous effects that kind of barely cover the central pop culture idea, but don't really cover the full implications OR establish this as its own thing. It's not a bad idea, and you do have good impulses for the mental imagery and human drama involved, including the drama of Foundation involvement, but this needs to (and could easily) go deeper, clearer, and weirder.

I think if the central anomaly were better developed, solutions for the problems I mention above would probably make themselves obvious.

For now, -1. I really hope you consider reworking it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a prior wrestling fan, I find the concept (and "Agent Lawler" XD) amusing enough that I like this

(Meh, this was meant as a separate comment and not a reply to this specific one)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm largely with shaggy and Abettik here. I feel like you didn't fully commit to the idea – it almost comes across as a lack of confidence in the execution. The central concept is really fun and interesting, especially considering that kayfabe is a unique example of what's essentially an irl perception-distorting memetic hazard, but it feels like you didn't give the idea the respect it deserves, if that makes sense.

For instance, there's no doubt in my mind that this anomaly is a Keter. It's so widespread and embedded in society that the containment procedure is to contain reality instead of the anomaly itself? That's a serious information breach. Classing it as Euclid is selling the anomaly short.

There was lots of opportunity to have fun with the transcripts, but they're all too short, and the changes between affected and unaffected versions are minimal and have weak impact. You needed more emotional language, I think. Tell us that the wrestlers getting mauled scream out in genuine pain, that they have a crazed rage in their eyes while fighting, that sort of thing. Really ham up the hatred in the interview transcript. The SCP format is terse, and logs provide your only chance to break free of clinical tone, so you need to make the most of it.

I also felt that you missed an opportunity to describe how the anomaly interacts with society at large – the wider ramifications of it affecting so many people. That would have helped give the piece depth and a sense of scale.

It's a shame. This was a good idea, and the writing is technically competent. I get the sense that there's nothing about your writing ability stopping you from delivering with more impact. Next time you write for the site – and I hope you do! – really go all out and have confidence in what you're writing. I have no doubt that you should.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It's hinted, but not clearly given example. "

Isn't that what nuance is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That time the Hulkster was sent into the underworld and trapped by the Dungeon of Doom, to put an end to Hulkamania? Real. Want evidence? Hulkamania ended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might make a little more sense to me if the anomaly is that a small number of people perceive something fake to be real, instead of the anomaly being that a large number of people perceive something real to be fake.

I had pretty much the same reaction though, which is why I downvoted. Maybe if that ambiguity were introduced, it could have been more interesting, but as it stands now I can't buy the premise of this; it wasn't really humorous or strange or horrifying enough to get over this hurdle. Structurally, the way it's handled leads to quite a bit of repetition that bloats the text to an unnecessary degree which left me skimming for the interesting bits, which were scant.

'Keyfabe as an SCP' feels like a great idea, but the way it's executed here leaves much to be desired. It feels like a waste of a perfectly good topic. I think a shift in the idea may help this to be more than a mild success. Depending on the type of reaction you were aiming for, I would suggest one of the following:

  • Keyfabe is merely complex containment procedures or ritual that keeps some epic being at bay. Now, this is kinda SCP-2090's shtick, but I think there's potential to twist this in enough ways so that it's sufficiently original.
  • Keyfabe is involuntary, and the SCPF is struggling to figure out how to free wrestlers from their internal hell. It could very well be that something is manipulating people akin to action figures.
  • Keyfabe is a trace-like state wrestlers abuse as a form of escapism.

Or hell, maybe all three of those things combined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1

This one doesn't really work for me. It's very clunky, and I can't wrap my head around how it would actually work. It might make a little more sense to me if the anomaly is that a small number of people perceive something fake to be real, instead of the anomaly being that a large number of people perceive something real to be fake. Like how does the audience retcon in their head all of the deaths and hospitalizations that invariably occur? And despite all of that, it's not particularly interesting or funny. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the concept, but I thought the execution was very bland. The logs just confirm what the description says rather than expanding on it, and I think you could have done more with the undercover agent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scp is the cream of the crop. And just like the cream, it will rise to the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll think about it. I'm going to follow this up with a series of stories about agents posing as wrestlers during their entire career and exploring the possibility this article opens up - what are the odds that the insanity of Real Wrestling hurts the world around it without us being able to perceive it, etc. I'm not trying to convince you to upvote this article by proxy, but if you want me to expand on this work, I think you'll enjoy that series when I get around to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I think if there were more of this — if only to push the idea that wrestling is real in the universe of this article — it might just be worth an upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Just like in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are the El Santo movies where he fights vampires and werewolves actually documentaries?

Headcanoned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the anomaly is cute, this really doesn't work for me from a writing standpoint.

You don't really ever clearly get across that kayfabe is the SCP. It's hinted, but not clearly given example. Even when explicitly stated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So here's a question; does that mean supernatural stuff that happens in wrestling is also real? Like, did The Ultimate Warrior really travel to the Terrain of Testimant through the power of Destrucity? Are the El Santo movies where he fights vampires and werewolves actually documentaries?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this sums up my impression. It's very on-the-nose, yet at the same time, the logs are pretty convincing. Likely have to give it some thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what to make of this. The anomaly, all things considered, is dull. Yet, the implications are not dull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wrestling = real as fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP, I like it. Another +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kayfabe as a SCP?! Shut up and take my +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wrestling is real and demands to be respected just like any other sport, jabroni.
